name: scala-pro
description: Master enterprise-grade Scala development with functional
capabilities: Design and implement enterprise Scala applications using functional programming patterns
Build distributed systems with Apache Pekko, Akka, and reactive streaming
Optimize Scala code performance with JVM tuning, profiling, and benchmarking
Implement domain-driven design patterns with algebraic data types and type-safe DSLs
Create type-safe REST and GraphQL APIs with Http4s, Tapir, and Play Framework
Develop big data pipelines using Apache Spark with DataFrame and RDD operations
author: sickn33
securitySummary: Prompt-only skill with no executable code. Static analysis scanned 0 files and detected 0 potential security issues. The skill contains only natural language instructions for Scala development guidance. No suspicious patterns, command execution, network requests, or prompt injection attempts detected. Risk score: 0/100.
valueStatement: Developers building Scala applications need expert guidance on functional programming patterns, distributed system architecture, and performance optimization. This skill provides enterprise-grade Scala development assistance for Akka, Spark, ZIO, and reactive architectures.
limitations: Does not execute or compile Scala code - provides guidance only
Cannot access external databases or runtime environments for testing
Limited to Scala ecosystem - does not cover Java, Kotlin, or other JVM languages
Cannot build or deploy applications - only provides architectural advice

bestPractices: Prefer immutable data structures and pure functions to maximize testability and reasoning about code behavior
Use type-driven development - leverage Scala's type system to enforce correctness at compile time
Separate effectful code from business logic using ZIO or Cats Effect layers for clean architecture
antiPatterns: Avoid mixing imperative and functional code - stick to one paradigm within each module
Do not use var or mutable collections in core business logic - use persistent data structures instead
Avoid catching generic Exceptions - always model errors explicitly with sum types or error channels

faq.question: What is the difference between ZIO and Cats Effect?
faq.answer: Both are functional effect systems for Scala. ZIO provides a built-in error channel and dependency injection via ZLayer. Cats Effect is more lightweight and integrates with Cats libraries. Choose ZIO for integrated error handling or Cats Effect for maximum library compatibility.

faq.question: When should I use Akka/Pekko versus Spark?
faq.answer: Use Akka/Pekko for building distributed applications with the Actor model, real-time streaming, and microservices. Use Apache Spark for batch processing, ETL pipelines, and big data analytics. They serve different purposes and can be used together.

faq.question: How do I migrate from Akka to Apache Pekko?
faq.answer: Apache Pekko is the open-source continuation of Akka. Migration involves updating dependencies from com.typesafe.akka to org.apache.pekko. Most APIs are compatible. Review the Pekko migration guide for breaking changes and use the Pekko Migration Kit for automated help.

faq.question: What are the best testing frameworks for Scala?
faq.answer: ScalaTest and Specs2 are the main testing frameworks. Use ScalaTest with FunSpec or FlatSpec for flexibility. ScalaCheck enables property-based testing. For effect systems, usezio-test and cats-effect-testkit for testing ZIO and Cats Effect code.

faq.question: How do I achieve type-safe database access in Scala?
faq.answer: Use Doobie for pure functional JDBC access, Slick for ORM-style queries, or Quill for compile-time verified SQL. Doobie provides the most control, Slick offers familiar ORM patterns, and Quill generates queries at compile time for maximum safety.

faq.question: What is the best build tool for Scala projects?
faq.answer: SBT is the most common and mature build tool with excellent plugin ecosystem. Mill is gaining popularity for faster builds and simpler configuration. Choose SBT for complex multi-module projects or Mill for faster iteration times.
